questions.scenario: A public repository accepts pull requests from forks. A contributor modifies the Jenkinsfile so a deploy stage posts `$AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY` to an external URL. Which Trust setting is the dangerous one?

options.text: Nobody, because Jenkins replaces fork PR Jenkinsfiles with the target branch's Jenkinsfile.
From users with Admin or Write permission, because random external contributors remain untrusted.
Everyone, because every fork PR Jenkinsfile is trusted and can control what the build runs.
Forks in the same account, because it blocks forks from personal accounts.

questions.explanation: The article's table says Everyone trusts all fork PR Jenkinsfiles and should never be used on a public repo. That setting gives an arbitrary contributor control over the pipeline code that might reach credentials.

questions.scenario: Which defenses match the article's guidance for fork pull requests?

options.text: Use a Trust level such as Nobody or From users with Admin or Write permission.
Gate deploy credentials behind `when { branch 'main' }` or an equivalent PR-safe condition.
Audit old multibranch projects so inherited settings are not trusting Everyone.
Rely on masking alone, because masking blocks network exfiltration.

questions.explanation: Trust settings decide whose Jenkinsfile may run, and pipeline-level gates keep credentials out of PR-time stages. Masking only redacts log output. It does not stop a malicious Jenkinsfile from sending data over the network.

questions.scenario: A maintainer wants every pull request to compile and test, but deployments should run only after reviewed code reaches `main`. Which Jenkinsfile pattern fits?

options.text: Keep Test ungated and gate Deploy with `when { branch 'main' }` before binding any deploy credentials.
Bind deploy credentials at the top level and let every stage decide whether to use them.
Trust Everyone so PR contributors can debug deploy failures themselves.
Disable PR builds entirely.

questions.explanation: The article's example keeps the test stage available to PRs and gates the deploy stage on `branch 'main'`. The credential binding lives inside the gated stage, so PR builds never receive the secret.

questions.scenario: A fork PR is configured with Trust set to Nobody. What Jenkinsfile should the PR build run?

options.text: The Jenkinsfile from the target branch on the origin repository.
The Jenkinsfile from the fork, because that is what the contributor edited.
No Jenkinsfile, because Nobody disables all PR feedback.
A Jenkinsfile generated from `$JENKINS_HOME/config.xml`.

questions.explanation: The article's Trust table says Nobody treats all fork PRs as untrusted and replaces their Jenkinsfile with the maintainer-controlled Jenkinsfile from the target branch.

questions.scenario: A legacy multibranch project was created years ago. No one remembers which fork Trust level it uses. What is the right maintenance action?

options.text: Audit Configure > Branch Sources > Discover pull requests from forks > Trust, and move public repos away from Everyone.
Ignore it because modern Jenkins forces every old job to the safest setting automatically.
Delete all fork PR support permanently.
Move all secrets to repository-level credentials so Trust no longer matters.

questions.explanation: The article explicitly recommends auditing inherited multibranch projects. Older setups may have unsafe Trust settings, and the fix is configuration, not moving secrets into a broader scope.
